Assignment 4 – Tree & Graph Visualization

Data Visualization NetworkX Matplotlib Python

Two datasets · Four layouts · Fully documented

Dataset 1 – Animal Taxonomy Tree

Dataset Description

A rooted hierarchy curated from the ITIS – Integrated Taxonomic Information System (itis.gov). Biological taxonomy is by definition a strict hierarchy with a single root and no cycles, making it an ideal tree dataset.

AttributeDetail
SourceITIS – itis.gov
TypeRooted directed tree (DAG)
Nodes (33)Taxonomic ranks: Kingdom → Class → Order → Family → Species
Edges (32)Parent → Child: "the child taxon belongs to the parent taxon"
Depth4 levels below root (5 total)
Why a tree?Strict single-parent hierarchy — no cycles, one root, leaf nodes are species
Layout 1 – Top-Down Hierarchical. Root (Animalia) at top; each row is one taxonomic level. Colour-coded by rank. Best for reading parent–child relationships explicitly.
Layout 2 – Radial. Root at centre; each concentric ring = one level. Compact and highlights branching breadth of each class.
LayoutStrengthsBest Used When
Top-Down Hierarchical Clear parent–child relationships; easy level-by-level reading Taxonomies, org charts, chains of command with few levels
Radial Compact; shows branching symmetry; saves horizontal space Deep trees with many leaves; comparing subtree breadth

Dataset 2 – Zachary's Karate Club Network

Dataset Description

A classic social network from Wayne Zachary's 1977 peer-reviewed study of a university karate club. The club split into two factions after a dispute between the instructor (Node 0) and club administrator (Node 33). The network is built into NetworkX (nx.karate_club_graph()).

AttributeDetail
SourceZachary, W.W. (1977). J. Anthropological Research; built into NetworkX
TypeUndirected, unweighted social graph
Nodes (34)Club members
Edges (78)Observed social interactions outside the club
Ground-truth labelsTwo factions: Mr. Hi (instructor) vs Officer (administrator)
Why a graph?Many-to-many connections, cycles, no root — a true general graph
Layout 1 – Spring (Fruchterman-Reingold). Simulates physical forces — edges as springs, nodes as charged particles. The two factions cluster naturally. Node size ∝ degree.
Layout 2 – Kamada-Kawai. Positions nodes to match graph-theoretic distances. Gold rings mark top-5 betweenness centrality nodes (inter-faction bridges).
LayoutStrengthsBest Used When
Spring (Fruchterman-Reingold) Organic clustering; reveals communities naturally; minimises edge crossings Community detection, social networks, exploratory analysis
Kamada-Kawai Geometrically faithful to graph distances; highlights bridge nodes Structural analysis, identifying cut vertices, centrality comparison

Key Observations

Tree Dataset

The hierarchical layout makes taxonomic levels immediately readable — Animalia at the top, species at the bottom. The radial layout reveals that Mammalia has the widest branching (most families and species), while Amphibia is the narrowest.

Graph Dataset

Both layouts expose the two factions (red vs blue) and identify Node 0 and Node 33 as dominant hubs. The Kamada-Kawai layout additionally highlights the bridge nodes (gold rings) — members who maintained friendships across both factions before the club split. These are the nodes with the highest betweenness centrality.

Assignment 4 · Data Visualization · GIKI · Generated with Python + NetworkX + Matplotlib
